blocks.text: Primary Care Provider and Transitional Care
5660 W Flamingo Rd Ste B, Las Vegas, NV 891032312
Visited on: 2026 Mar 19 11:00 (Age at visit: 67 years)
Electronically signed by: JEFFERY OLSEN, DO on 2026-03-24 11:33 AM
Chief Complaint: Recent fall at home.
MEDICAL NECESSITY: This visit was a face-to-face encounter with the patient in her
home. This is not a visit of convenience. The patient is seen in the home because there is
a medical necessity for this visit to be conducted in the patient's environment. The patient
suffers from progressive dementia, severe mobility limitations, and a recent fall, which
are contraindications for the patient to be seen in an office setting today. It is medically
necessary and safer for the patient to be seen in her own environment today.
Delores D. is an elderly female with a history of progressive dementia, type 2 diabetes
mellitus, atrial fibrillation, COPD, bipolar disorder, and nephrolithiasis who presents for
routine follow-up care. The patient has multiple comorbidities and cognitive function that is
The patient recently completed a hospital stay and required 5 days of CNA assistance
because she was unable to shower independently. She experienced one fall approximately
2 weeks ago when she missed the doorknob while trying to exit and fell to the floor. The
patient has a significant smoking history of 30-40 years, previously averaging 2 packs per
day, but reports she quit smoking one month ago and is currently using nicotine patches.
She denies any current cigarette use.
The patient reports sleeping well and denies any current pain, including abdominal pain,
chest pain, or arm pain. She states she feels good overall. The patient has hard of hearing
and may be getting hearing aids pending approval. She lives with family support, with her
daughter Rochelle providing care assistance. The patient denies alcohol use and reports
no behavioral issues or wounds on her body.
Her last blood work was completed in September of the previous year. The patient is
described as loving to eat and continues on diabetic medications but is not currently on
insulin, though she was previously on insulin therapy.
General: Reports persistent sleepiness and increased appetite.
HEENT: Hard of hearing; no hearing aid in place.
Respiratory: Known COPD; no acute dyspnea mentioned.
Musculoskeletal: Recent single fall; ambulation decline; no current pain.
Skin: No wounds noted per patient/caregiver.
Neuro/Psych: Progressive dementia; no behavioral disturbances; alert & oriented x2
(Medication reconciliation last updated 04/20/2025 07:55 AM, JEE JEAN DYKHENG -
pioglitazone 30 mg tablet, Take 1 tablet by mouth once daily
digoxin 125 mcg (0.125 mg) tablet, Take 1 tablet by mouth daily
FIASP FLEX TOUCH PEN INJ, INJECT 15 UNITS SUBCUTANEOUSLY IN THE
MORNING AND 15 UNITS IN THE EVENING AND 15 UNITS BEFORE BEDTIME
Vitamin D3 125 mcg (5,000 unit) tablet, 1 tablet daily

The patients speech was normal, sharing conversation with normal laryngeal efforts.
Appropriate mood and affect were seen on exam. Thought processes were logical,
relevant, and thoughts were completed normally. Thought content was normal. Thought
content was normal with no psychotic or suicidal thoughts. The patients judgement was
realistic with normal insight into their present condition. Mental status included: correct
time, place, person orientation, normal recent and remote memory, normal attention span
and concentration ability. Language skills included the ability to correctly name objects.
Fund of knowledge included normal awareness of current and past events.
Reviewed HRA. Copy of HRA in documents section.
1. Does the patient need help with the phone, transportation, shopping, preparing meals,
housework, laundry, medications or managing money? Yes
2. Does the patient's home have rugs in the hallway, lack grab bars in the bathroom, lack
handrails on the stairs or have poor lighting? Yes
3. Has the patient noticed any hearing difficulties? No
Fall Screening: timed "Up and Go" test less than 30 seconds. Cognitive Test performed is
MMSE and the result is Normal.
RESP: lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales, wheezes or rhonchi, nonlabored
breathing, no use of accessory of muscles of respiration
GI: +BS, nontender to palpation, no masses, no HSM

Patient has inability to leave home and consequently leaving home would require a
- Refer to home health services including PT, nursing and CNA care- Continue nicotine
- Monitor respiratory status; no acute exacerbation today.
- Reassess need for pulmonary follow-up if symptoms change.
For worsening symptoms, call 911 or go to ER - Pt/CG verbalized understanding.
Follow-up: 4-6 weeks or PRN. Report acute changes.
- Financial, privacy, and consent to treat notices are signed and understood by patient/CG.
- It was explained to the patient/CG that the patient is permitted to have only one PCP or
transitional care/gap provider at any given time. If the patient has more than one PCP or
at any given time, insurance may not cover and patient is responsible for the bill/coverage.
Falls: Risk for falls and fall prevention reviewed with Pt and family member. Falls can lead
to severe injuries and morbidity that may lead to inpatient hospitalizations. Utilize assistive
decrease clutter, especially in the walkway, and rise slowly. Ask for help when needed.
Patients social history, medical history, family history, surgical history, and medication
(current and past) was reviewed. Patient's current health status, prognosis, morbidity, and
considered. Patients plan of care was carefully and clinically considered and evaluated for
the best outcome for the Pt.

ACP- Discussed goals for medical treatment f2f for 30min. Patient comfortable following
the provider's recommendation, which is guided by her explicit preferences. Advance care
component of shared decision making and although it is not required to, may result
in documentation of a living will, assignment of a health care proxy, or other advance
CCM- Comprehensive personalized chronic care plan created, revised, and updated, as
needed. Provided a comprehensive care plan to set and track progress towards health
goals, and support between regular face- to-face visits. Spent 30min with patient.
Spent time 45min with Health behavior assessment including evaluation of the patient's
responses to disease, illness or injury, outlook, coping strategies, motivation, and
This encounter is medically necessary due to the change in the patients condition or
Pts assessment, evaluation and care plan is of moderate to high complexity.
Questions and concerns addressed with Pt/CG - verbalized understanding.
- Monitor for worsening symptoms or new onset of weakness
- Assess for reversible causes (e.g., anemia, infection, medication side effects) with labs as
- Reinforce importance of adequate hydration and nutrition
- Family to pursue hearing aid; awaiting insurance approval.
- Avoid exposure to respiratory irritants (smoke, dust, strong fumes)
- Take all medications as prescribed, including inhalers and nicotine patches
- Monitor for increased shortness of breath, cough, or sputum changes
- Practice breathing exercises as instructed by your provider or physical therapist
- Stay up to date with vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal)
- Contact your healthcare provider if you experience worsening symptoms or signs of
- Take all heart medications as prescribed (e.g., digoxin, metoprolol)
- Monitor for symptoms such as palpitations, dizziness, chest pain, or shortness of breath
- Maintain regular follow-up appointments and lab monitoring as directed
- Stay hydrated and avoid excessive caffeine or alcohol
- Notify your provider if you experience new or worsening symptoms

- Understand the importance of medication adherence to reduce risk of stroke and
- Report any new or worsening muscle weakness to your provider promptly

- Avoid activities that increase fall risk if you feel weak
- Monitor for associated symptoms such as pain, numbness, or tingling
